topics.title: Metals for Implants - Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT614 - BIOMATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metals for Implants - Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Metallic Biomaterials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metallic Biomaterials** are inorganic, polycrystalline, metallic alloys specifically engineered to interface with biological systems (hard or soft tissue) for the purpose of evaluating, treating, augmenting, or replacing any tissue, organ, or function of the body, **without causing adverse biological reactions** during the intended clinical service life.

Metals have been used as implant materials for over a century. Unlike ceramics and polymers, metals possess a unique combination of high **tensile strength**, **ductility**, **fracture toughness**, and **fatigue resistance**, making them indispensable for load-bearing applications such as orthopedic fixation devices (bone plates, screws), total joint replacements (hip stems, femoral components), dental implants, and cardiovascular stents.

## 1.2 Why Metals? The Need for Metallic Implants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Human cortical bone has a Young's Modulus of approximately **10–30 GPa** and an Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) of about **50–150 MPa**. Metals, with UTS often exceeding **500 MPa**, provide the necessary mechanical support that polymers and ceramics cannot match in bulk-load scenarios.

Metals are preferred over other biomaterial classes for the following reasons:
- **High Load-Bearing Capacity:** They can withstand physiological cyclic loads (walking, chewing) for millions of cycles without failure.
- **Ductility:** They can be deformed, shaped, and machined into complex geometries (e.g., porous structures, thin wires).
- **Electrical Conductivity:** Useful in specific applications like neural stimulation electrodes.
- **Sterilizability:** They can withstand high-temperature autoclaving without degradation.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: "The Steel Skeleton of a Building"**
> Imagine a multi-story building. You cannot use wood (polymers) or glass/brick (ceramics) for the main load-bearing columns if you want a 50-story skyscraper. You need **steel reinforcement (metal)**. Similarly, when a human hip joint is broken, the body needs a strong, tough, and fatigue-resistant "structural column" to replace the femur head. Polymers would wear out in months, and ceramics would shatter under a sudden fall. Metals provide that structural backbone.

## 1.4 General Property Requirements for a Metallic Implant

For any metal to qualify as a successful implant material, it must satisfy a strict design envelope. These are generally categorized as follows:

| Property Category | Specific Requirements |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Biocompatibility** | Non-toxic, non-allergenic, non-carcinogenic, no inflammatory response |
| **Mechanical** | High fatigue strength, low modulus (closer to bone ~ 110 GPa or less ideally), high wear resistance |
| **Chemical** | Excellent corrosion resistance in physiological saline (~ 0.9% NaCl at 37°C, pH 7.4) |
| **Manufacturability** | Can be cast, forged, machined, and polished to mirror finishes |
| **Osseointegration** | Ability to bond with surrounding bone (or be bioactively coated) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common mistake students make is stating that the ideal implant material should be **chemically inert**. While noble metals like Gold (Au) and Platinum (Pt) are inert, they are mechanically too soft. Modern philosophy leans towards **"biocompatible but reactive"** — metals that form a stable, passive oxide layer (e.g., TiO₂ on Titanium) which controls the ion release rate, rather than being completely inert.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Metals for Implants

Metallic implant materials can be classified using multiple criteria. The KTU 2024 syllabus focuses on the classification based on **Tissue Response** and **Mechanical/Corrosion Behavior**.

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Tissue Response (Biocompatibility)

This is the most clinically relevant classification, originally proposed by Huggins and refined by later researchers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Tiers of Metallic Biocompatibility:**
> 1. **Noble Metals (Almost Inert):** Pure Gold (Au), Platinum (Pt), Tantalum (Ta), Iridium (Ir). They resist corrosion exceptionally well but are mechanically inadequate for load-bearing.
> 2. **Passivating / Surface-Reactive Metals:** Titanium (Ti) and its alloys, Tantalum (Ta), Niobium (Nb), Zirconium (Zr). They form a highly stable, adherent, and protective oxide layer (e.g., TiO₂) that spontaneously reforms if damaged (self-healing passivation).
> 3. **Non-Passivating / Corrodible Metals:** Iron (Fe), Magnesium (Mg), Zinc (Zn), and their alloys. They corrode in the physiological environment. While historically avoided, they are now the foundation of **Biodegradable Stents** (e.g., Mg-based scaffolds).

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Functional Application

| Implant Type | Examples | Typical Metal Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Load-Bearing (Permanent)** | Hip stem, knee joint, dental root | Co-Cr-Mo, Ti-6Al-4V |
| **Fixation Devices (Temporary)** | Bone plates, screws, pins | Stainless Steel (316L) |
| **Cardiovascular** | Stents, heart valve frames | Nitinol (Ni-Ti), 316L SS, Co-Cr |
| **Dental Prosthetics** | Crowns, bridges, dentures | Au alloys, Co-Cr, Ti |
| **Biodegradable / Temporary** | Cardiovascular stents, bone screws | Mg alloys, Fe alloys, Zn alloys |

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) Compatibility

This is a critical modern classification for post-operative imaging.
- **Ferromagnetic (MRI Conditional/Hazardous):** Austenitic stainless steel (slightly), Co-Cr alloys. They cause image artifacts and may experience torque in the strong MRI magnetic field.
- **Non-Ferromagnetic (MRI Safe):** Titanium and its alloys, Tantalum, Nitinol. They are the preferred choice for patients who may require frequent MRI scans.

## 2.2 Deep Dive: The "Big Three" Implant Alloys

The vast majority (over 90%) of metallic implants worldwide are manufactured from one of these three alloy families.

### 2.2.1 Stainless Steel (Specifically 316L)
- **Composition:** Iron (~65%), Chromium (~17%), Nickel (~12%), Molybdenum (~2%), and trace carbon (< 0.03% — hence the **'L'** denoting Low carbon).
- **Function of Alloying Elements:**
  - **Cr (≥16%):** Forms a passive Cr₂O₃ layer.
  - **Ni:** Stabilizes the Austenite (FCC) phase at room temperature, ensuring non-magnetic behavior and ductility.
  - **Mo:** Enhances pitting corrosion resistance in chloride-rich (body fluid) environments.
  - **Low C:** Prevents precipitation of chromium carbides at grain boundaries, which would cause "sensitization" and intergranular corrosion.
- **Disadvantage:** Contains Nickel, which is a known allergen (Ni-induced dermatitis). Long-term ion release remains a concern.

### 2.2.2 Cobalt-Chromium (Co-Cr) Alloys
- **Composition:** Cobalt (~60–65%), Chromium (~25–30%), Molybdenum (~5–7%), and trace Nickel.
- **Properties:** Extremely high wear resistance, high strength, and excellent corrosion resistance due to a robust Cr₂O₃ passive film.
- **Disadvantage:** Very high elastic modulus (~ 200–240 GPa), leading to **stress shielding** — the bone is not loaded sufficiently because the implant is too stiff, leading to bone resorption and eventual implant loosening.

### 2.2.3 Titanium and its Alloys
- **Commercially Pure Titanium (CP-Ti):** Grades 1–4. Used for dental implants due to excellent osseointegration.
- **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5):** The workhorse alloy. Alpha-beta phase. Contains Aluminum (alpha stabilizer) and Vanadium (beta stabilizer).
- **Advantage:** Lowest elastic modulus among the Big Three (~ 110 GPa), excellent biocompatibility, forms a very stable TiO₂ passive layer.
- **Concerns:** Vanadium is cytotoxic. Newer beta-Ti alloys (e.g., Ti-Nb-Zr-Sn, often called "TNZS") use non-toxic elements like Niobium and Zirconium.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Key Numerical Benchmarks

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles the **High-Yield Constants and Equations** frequently asked in KTU Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions regarding implant metals.

| Parameter / Formula | Symbolic Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Young's Modulus (Bone)** | $E_{bone}$ | **10–30 GPa** | Cortical bone stiffness |
| **Young's Modulus (316L SS)** | $E_{SS}$ | **~ 190 GPa** | High stiffness → Stress Shielding |
| **Young's Modulus (Co-Cr)** | $E_{CoCr}$ | **~ 210–240 GPa** | Highest stiffness in implant metals |
| **Young's Modulus (Ti-6Al-4V)** | $E_{Ti}$ | **~ 110 GPa** | Closest to bone, preferred |
| **Modular Ratio (Stress Shielding Indicator)** | $R_m = E_{implant} \div E_{bone}$ | Dimensionless | Ideal ratio approaches 1 |
| **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** | $R_{PB} = \dfrac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}}$ | $1 < R_{PB} < 2$ is ideal | If PBR < 1, oxide is porous; if > 2, oxide spalls off |
| **Corrosion Rate (Penetration)** | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | mm/year | Faraday's constant $K$ used; $EW$ is equivalent weight |
| **Passivation Potential** | $E_{pp}$ | ~ 0.5 V vs SCE (for SS) | Voltage at which passive film forms |
| **Fatigue Limit (Endurance Limit)** | $\sigma_{e}$ | ~ 0.4 to 0.5 of UTS | Stress below which life is infinite |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** Remember the modular ratio for a **316L SS hip stem**:
> $$R_m = \frac{190 \text{ GPa}}{20 \text{ GPa}} \approx 9.5$$
> This means the steel is **~10 times stiffer** than the bone it replaces, severely disrupting Wolff's Law (bone remodels in response to mechanical load).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification of metals directly dictates **implant design strategy**:
1. A **fracture fixation plate** is often made of 316L SS because it is temporary (removed in 12–18 months), cost-effective, and high strength is required.
2. A **permanent hip prosthesis** uses Ti-6Al-4V or Co-Cr for longevity.
3. **Cardiovascular stents** use 316L SS or Co-Cr (for balloon-expandable) and Nitinol (for self-expandable) due to their unique shape memory and superelasticity.
4. **Pediatric implants** ideally use biodegradable Mg alloys so that the implant disappears as the child's bone grows, avoiding multiple revision surgeries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Implant Metals

The following matrix is a **mandatory comparative tool** that KTU examiners expect students to draw/memorize for 14-mark questions.

| Property | 316L Stainless Steel | Co-Cr-Mo Alloy | Ti-6Al-4V Alloy | Nitinol (Ni-Ti) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Use** | Temporary fixation | Joint replacement, dental | Permanent implants, dental | Stents, orthodontic wires |
| **Density (g/cm³)** | ~ 7.9 | ~ 8.3 | ~ 4.5 | ~ 6.5 |
| **Young's Modulus (GPa)** | ~ 190 | ~ 240 | ~ 110 | ~ 30–80 (variable) |
| **UTS (MPa)** | 490–690 | 655–1500 | 860–1100 | 700–1500 (austenite) |
| **Elongation (%)** | 30–40 | 8–20 | 10–15 | 10–25 |
| **Biocompatibility** | Moderate (Ni release) | Good (Ni, Co release) | Excellent (V concerns) | Good (Ni concerns) |
| **MRI Compatibility** | Conditional (artifacts) | Conditional (artifacts) | Excellent (non-mag) | Excellent (non-mag) |
| **Relative Cost** | Low | High | Moderate | Very High |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Estimating the Modular Ratio (Stress Shielding Quantification)

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Sub-Part):**
A hip prosthesis is to be designed using a cylindrical metallic stem. Compare the modular mismatch (stress shielding potential) for a 316L Stainless Steel stem versus a Ti-6Al-4V stem against a host cortical bone of Young's Modulus $E_{bone} = 20$ GPa. Justify which material is superior from a biomechanical perspective.

### Given Data:
- $E_{316L} = 190$ GPa
- $E_{Ti6Al4V} = 110$ GPa
- $E_{bone} = 20$ GPa

### Step 1: Define the Modular Ratio
The modular ratio is defined as the ratio of the Young's Modulus of the implant material to that of the host bone.

$$R_m = \frac{E_{implant}}{E_{bone}}$$

> A ratio $R_m$ close to 1 indicates a biomechanically compatible implant. A high ratio indicates excessive stiffness.

### Step 2: Calculate $R_m$ for 316L Stainless Steel
Substitute the values into the equation:

$$R_{m,SS} = \frac{E_{316L}}{E_{bone}} = \frac{190 \text{ GPa}}{20 \text{ GPa}}$$

$$R_{m,SS} = 9.5$$

**Conclusion for SS:** The 316L stem is **9.5 times stiffer** than the bone.

### Step 3: Calculate $R_m$ for Ti-6Al-4V
Substitute the values into the equation:

$$R_{m,Ti} = \frac{E_{Ti6Al4V}}{E_{bone}} = \frac{110 \text{ GPa}}{20 \text{ GPa}}$$

$$R_{m,Ti} = 5.5$$

**Conclusion for Ti:** The Ti-6Al-4V stem is **5.5 times stiffer** than the bone.

### Step 4: Biomechanical Justification
Since $R_{m,Ti} < R_{m,SS}$ (i.e., 5.5 < 9.5), the Ti-6Al-4V stem exhibits significantly less mismatch with the bone. In an iso-strain composite model (assuming bone and implant deform together), the load carried by each constituent is proportional to its stiffness. Therefore, the Ti-6Al-4V implant allows more physiological load to be transferred to the adjacent bone, reducing **stress shielding-induced bone resorption**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Although Ti-6Al-4V is biomechanically superior, in KTU answers you must always mention the **counter-argument**: for very heavy patients or highly demanding mechanical environments, the lower absolute modulus of Ti may not provide sufficient rigidity to prevent implant micromotion, and Co-Cr (despite high stress shielding) might be clinically necessary. This shows the examiner you understand engineering trade-offs.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Pilling-Bedworth Ratio for Passive Oxide Stability

**Problem Statement:**
For a Titanium implant, the oxide (TiO₂) layer protects the metal from further corrosion. The Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR) determines if this oxide is protective. Given:
- Molar mass of Ti = 47.87 g/mol
- Density of Ti metal = 4.51 g/cm³
- Molar mass of TiO₂ = 79.87 g/mol
- Density of TiO₂ = 4.26 g/cm³
Calculate the PBR and comment on the protectiveness of the oxide.

### Step 1: Theoretical Definition
The PBR is the ratio of the molar volume of the metal oxide to the molar volume of the parent metal.

$$R_{PB} = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \frac{(M_{oxide} / \rho_{oxide})}{(M_{metal} / \rho_{metal})}$$

### Step 2: Substitute Numerical Values
$$R_{PB} = \frac{79.87 / 4.26}{47.87 / 4.51}$$

### Step 3: Evaluate Numerator
$$\frac{79.87}{4.26} = 18.75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate Denominator
$$\frac{47.87}{4.51} = 10.61 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}$$

### Step 5: Final Ratio
$$R_{PB} = \frac{18.75}{10.61} \approx 1.77$$

### Step 6: Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Rule of Pilling and Bedworth:**
> - If $R_{PB} < 1$: The oxide is too thin/porous. It cannot cover the metal surface completely. **Not protective.**
> - If $1 < R_{PB} < 2$: The oxide volume is sufficient to cover the surface, and compressive stresses develop within the oxide, making it adherent, dense, and **protective.**
> - If $R_{PB} > 2$: The oxide is too bulky, develops high compressive stress, cracks, and spalls off. **Not protective (e.g., Iron, $R_{PB} \approx 2.15$).**

Since $1 < 1.77 < 2$, the TiO₂ layer is **highly protective**, which is the fundamental reason why Titanium is the most corrosion-resistant structural implant metal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Tree (Mermaid Diagram)

The following diagram provides a visual taxonomic map of metallic implant materials, following the classification logic established in Section 2.

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
```mermaid

    A[Metals for Implants] --> B[Classification Basis 1: Tissue Response]
    A --> C[Classification Basis 2: Functional Application]
    A --> D[Classification Basis 3: MRI Compatibility]

    B --> B1[Noble or Almost Inert Metals]
    B --> B2[Passivating or Surface Reactive Metals]
    B --> B3[Non-Passivating or Corrodible Metals]

    B1 --> B1a[Gold Au]
    B1 --> B1b[Platinum Pt]
    B1 --> B1c[Tantalum Ta]
    B1 --> B1d[Iridium Ir]

    B2 --> B2a[Titanium CP-Ti and Ti Alloys]
    B2 --> B2b[Tantalum Ta]
    B2 --> B2c[Niobium Nb]
    B2 --> B2d[Zirconium Zr]
    B2 --> B2e[316L Stainless Steel Passivates]
    B2 --> B2f[Co-Cr Alloys Passivates]

    B3 --> B3a[Pure Iron Fe]
    B3 --> B3b[Magnesium Mg Alloys]
    B3 --> B3c[Zinc Zn Alloys]

    C --> C1[Load Bearing Permanent]
    C --> C2[Temporary Fixation]
    C --> C3[Cardiovascular Stents]
    C --> C4[Biodegradable Scaffolds]

    C1 --> C1a[Hip and Knee Prosthesis - Co-Cr, Ti-6Al-4V]
    C2 --> C2a[Bone Plates and Screws - 316L SS]
    C3 --> C3a[Ni-Ti Nitinol, 316L SS, Co-Cr]
    C4 --> C4a[Mg-Zn Alloys, Fe-Mn Alloys]

    D --> D1[Ferromagnetic MRI Hazard]
    D --> D2[Non-Ferromagnetic MRI Safe]

    D1 --> D1a[Martensitic Stainless Steel]
    D1 --> D1b[Co-Cr Alloys Slight]
    D2 --> D2a[316L SS Austenitic]
    D2 --> D2b[Titanium and Ti Alloys]
    D2 --> D2c[Nitinol]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Metallic Implant Surface

The following block diagram illustrates the multi-layered functional architecture that governs the interaction between a metallic implant and the physiological environment.

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Physiological Environment: 0.9 percent NaCl, 37 degree C, pH 7.4] --> L2[Surface Oxide Passive Layer: TiO2 or Cr2O3]
    L2 --> L3[Bulk Metal Substrate: 316L SS, Co-Cr, or Ti-6Al-4V]
    L3 --> L4[Mechanical Load Transfer: Wolff Law Compliance]
    L2 --> L5[Ion Release Control: Passive Film Barrier]
    L5 --> L6[Tissue Interface: Osseointegration or Fibrous Tissue]
    L4 --> L6
    L6 --> L7[Long Term Clinical Outcome: Success or Failure]

    style L1 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style L2 fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style L3 fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#000000
    style L7 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix Flowchart for Implant Material Selection

This flowchart provides an engineering decision-making framework for selecting a metallic implant material based on clinical requirements.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Is the implant temporary or permanent?}
    Q1 -- Temporary less than 2 years --> Q2{Is cost a primary constraint?}
    Q1 -- Permanent greater than 10 years --> Q3{Is the site load-bearing?}

    Q2 -- Yes --> A1[Select 316L Stainless Steel]
    Q2 -- No --> A2[Select Ti-6Al-4V for better biocompatibility]

    Q3 -- Yes High load --> Q4{Patient weight and activity level?}
    Q3 -- No Low load e.g. dental --> A3[Select CP-Ti for excellent osseointegration]

    Q4 -- High --> A4[Select Co-Cr-Mo for maximum wear resistance]
    Q4 -- Moderate --> A5[Select Ti-6Al-4V for low stress shielding]

    style Q1 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style A1 fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366
    style A4 fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366
    style A5 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this in your KTU exam:** Drawing a labeled block diagram of the **"Bulk Metal → Passive Oxide Layer → Physiological Environment"** interface in a 7-mark answer immediately earns you 2 marks for visualization, even before you start writing descriptive text.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer & Definitions)

> **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define the term "Biocompatibility" in the context of metallic implant materials. Differentiate between "inert" and "passivating" metals with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
* **Definition (1 Mark):** Biocompatibility is the ability of a material to perform its desired function in the host tissue without eliciting any undesirable local or systemic effects, such as toxicity, allergic reaction, inflammation, or carcinogenesis.
* **Inert Metals (1 Mark):** These are noble metals that do not react or form a surface layer in physiological environments. Example: **Gold (Au)** or **Platinum (Pt)**. They are corrosion-resistant but mechanically soft.
* **Passivating Metals (1 Mark):** These are reactive metals that spontaneously form a thin, adherent, and protective oxide layer (passive film) on their surface which prevents further corrosion. Example: **Titanium (Ti)** which forms TiO₂.

---

> **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Why is "Low Carbon" content specified in 316L stainless steel used for surgical implants? What is the maximum permitted carbon percentage?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
* **Reason (2 Marks):** In austenitic stainless steel, carbon tends to react with chromium to form chromium carbides (Cr₂₃C₆) that precipitate at the grain boundaries. This depletes the adjacent matrix of chromium (a phenomenon called **"sensitization"**), destroying the passive Cr₂O₃ layer and causing **intergranular corrosion**. Low carbon prevents this sensitization.
* **Maximum Carbon (1 Mark):** The maximum carbon content in 316L is specified as **≤ 0.03%** (often written as 0.030 wt%).

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an internal choice. The question typically has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a) Classify metallic implant materials based on their tissue response. Provide suitable examples and clinical applications for each class. (7 Marks)**
>
> **(b) Explain the phenomenon of "Stress Shielding" in total hip replacement. Derive the modular ratio for a Ti-6Al-4V implant and a 316L SS implant against cortical bone ($E_{bone} = 20$ GPa) and justify which material is biomechanically superior. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

Metallic implants are classified into three categories based on their chemical reactivity in physiological tissue (as proposed by Huggins):

1. **Noble / Almost Inert Metals (2 Marks):**
   - They do not undergo chemical reactions in the body.
   - **Examples:** Gold (Au), Platinum (Pt), Iridium (Ir), Tantalum (Ta).
   - **Applications:** Electrodes in pacemakers, dental wires, and coatings. They are not used as bulk load-bearing implants due to low mechanical strength and high cost.

2. **Passivating / Surface-Reactive Metals (2.5 Marks):**
   - They are reactive but form a thin, stable, adherent, and self-healing passive oxide layer that isolates the bulk metal from the corrosive physiological environment.
   - **Examples:** Titanium and its alloys (TiO₂), Tantalum (Ta₂O₅), Niobium (Nb₂O₅), Zirconium (ZrO₂), and also 316L SS (Cr₂O₃) and Co-Cr alloys.
   - **Applications:** Orthopedic implants (femoral stems, bone plates), dental implants, and cardiovascular devices.

3. **Non-Passivating / Corrodible Metals (2.5 Marks):**
   - They actively corrode and degrade in physiological environments, releasing ions at a controlled rate.
   - **Examples:** Pure Iron (Fe), Magnesium (Mg) alloys, Zinc (Zn) alloys.
   - **Applications:** Biodegradable bone screws, cardiovascular stents (e.g., Mg-Zn-Y), and tissue engineering scaffolds where the implant is expected to dissolve after the tissue has healed.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

* **Concept of Stress Shielding (2 Marks):** According to **Wolff's Law**, bone remodels itself based on the magnitude of mechanical stress it experiences. When a metallic implant (hip stem) with a Young's Modulus significantly higher than the surrounding bone is inserted, it carries most of the physiological load. The adjacent bone, being under-loaded, undergoes resorption (bone loss), leading to implant loosening and eventual failure. This biomechanical mismatch is called **Stress Shielding**.

* **Calculation for Ti-6Al-4V (2 Marks):**
  $$R_{m,Ti} = \frac{E_{Ti6Al4V}}{E_{bone}} = \frac{110 \text{ GPa}}{20 \text{ GPa}} = 5.5$$

* **Calculation for 316L SS (1.5 Marks):**
  $$R_{m,SS} = \frac{E_{316L}}{E_{bone}} = \frac{190 \text{ GPa}}{20 \text{ GPa}} = 9.5$$

* **Justification (1.5 Marks):** Since $R_{m,Ti} (5.5) < R_{m,SS} (9.5)$, the **Ti-6Al-4V** stem is **biomechanically superior** because its elastic modulus is closer to that of the host bone. It allows more physiological load to be transferred to the femur, reducing the risk of stress shielding-induced bone resorption and ensuring longer implant longevity. However, for very high-load patients, Co-Cr alloys may still be chosen for their superior wear resistance despite the higher stress shielding.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a) With neat sketches/flowcharts, describe the classification of metallic implant materials based on (i) functional application and (ii) MRI compatibility. (7 Marks)**
>
> **(b) What is the Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR)? Calculate the PBR for the formation of Al₂O₃ on Aluminum and comment on the protectiveness of the oxide film. Given: M(Al) = 27 g/mol, ρ(Al) = 2.7 g/cm³, M(Al₂O₃) = 102 g/mol, ρ(Al₂O₃) = 3.99 g/cm³. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

* **Classification based on Functional Application (3.5 Marks):** Metals are selected based on the clinical role and the duration of implantation.
  - **Temporary Fixation Devices:** Bone plates, screws, intramedullary nails → 316L Stainless Steel (cost-effective, removed after healing).
  - **Permanent Load-Bearing Prostheses:** Hip stems, knee femoral components → Co-Cr-Mo and Ti-6Al-4V (high fatigue strength).
  - **Cardiovascular Implants:** Stents, heart valve housings → 316L SS, Co-Cr, Nitinol (Ni-Ti) (superelasticity).
  - **Dental Implants:** Endosseous root forms → Commercially Pure Titanium (CP-Ti) (excellent osseointegration).
  - **Biodegradable Implants:** Pediatric bone screws, vascular scaffolds → Mg alloys, Fe-Mn alloys.

* **Classification based on MRI Compatibility (3.5 Marks):**
  - **Ferromagnetic (MRI Conditional/Hazardous):** Martensitic stainless steels, and to a lesser extent, Co-Cr alloys. They are attracted by the strong magnetic field of an MRI machine, can cause projectile injury, and produce severe image artifacts.
  - **Non-Ferromagnetic (MRI Safe):** 316L SS (austenitic), Titanium and all its alloys, Tantalum, and Nitinol. They are the preferred choice for patients who require long-term post-operative MRI monitoring.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

* **Concept of PBR (3 Marks):** The Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR) is the ratio of the molar volume of the metal oxide to the molar volume of the parent metal from which the oxide is formed. It determines whether an oxide film is protective against further corrosion.

$$R_{PB} = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \frac{(M_{oxide} / \rho_{oxide})}{(M_{metal} / \rho_{metal})}$$

* **Calculation (3 Marks):**
  $$R_{PB} = \frac{(102 \text{ g/mol}) / (3.99 \text{ g/cm}^3)}{(27 \text{ g/mol}) / (2.7 \text{ g/cm}^3)}$$

  Numerator: $102 / 3.99 = 25.56$ cm³/mol
  Denominator: $27 / 2.7 = 10.00$ cm³/mol

  $$R_{PB} = \frac{25.56}{10.00} = 2.556$$

* **Comment on Protectiveness (1 Mark):** Since $R_{PB} = 2.556$, which is **significantly greater than 2**, the aluminum oxide layer is **not highly protective** against long-term corrosion. The oxide layer develops excessive compressive internal stresses, which causes it to crack, spall off, and expose the underlying metal. (Note: In reality, Al₂O₃ is kinetically protective due to slow ion diffusion, but thermodynamically the PBR > 2 indicates mechanical instability of the film.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "Inert" with "Non-Toxic":** Writing that Noble metals like Gold are "non-toxic" is correct, but saying they are "strong" is wrong. KTU examiners specifically deduct 0.5 marks if you don't mention the mechanical weakness of inert metals.
> 2. **Units in PBR:** The PBR is a **dimensionless ratio**. Students often forget to write the unit "cm³/mol" for the molar volumes in intermediate steps. Always explicitly write the units.
> 3. **Interchanging 316L and 316:** Many students write "316 stainless steel" in exams. The "**L**" (Low Carbon, <0.03%) is a mandatory specification. Writing 316 will cost you 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the Passive Layer:** When asked "Why is Titanium corrosion-resistant?", do not just say "because it is reactive." You MUST mention the **self-healing, passive TiO₂ layer**. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for the word "passivation."

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for the Exam:**

- **The Big Three:** 90% of load-bearing implants are 316L SS, Co-Cr-Mo, or Ti-6Al-4V. Memorize their composition, modulus, and primary use.
- **316L SS Composition:** Fe (matrix), Cr (≥16%, passivation), Ni (austenite stabilizer, ~12%), Mo (~2%, pitting resistance), C (<0.03%, low carbon).
- **Co-Cr-Mo:** Stiffest (E ≈ 240 GPa). Best for wear resistance. Contains Ni (allergen risk).
- **Ti-6Al-4V:** Closest modulus to bone (E ≈ 110 GPa). Best biocompatibility. Forms TiO₂. V is cytotoxic (newer alloys use Nb, Zr).
- **Nitinol (Ni-Ti):** Shape memory and superelasticity. Used for self-expanding stents. Equal atomic ratio of Ni and Ti.
- **Biocompatibility Tiers:** (1) Inert (Au, Pt), (2) Passivating (Ti, Ta), (3) Corrodible/Biodegradable (Mg, Zn, Fe).
- **Pilling-Bedworth Rule:** $1 < R_{PB} < 2$ = protective oxide. $< 1$ = porous. $> 2$ = spalls off.
- **Stress Shielding Rule:** $R_m = E_{implant} / E_{bone}$. Lower $R_m$ is biomechanically better.
- **MRI Rule:** Ti and Nitinol = MRI Safe. Martensitic SS = MRI Hazard. 316L SS = MRI Conditional.
- **Mnemonic for "L" in 316L:** **L**ow Carbon to prevent **S**ensitization (chromium carbide precipitation and intergranular corrosion).
- **Bone Properties:** Cortical bone E = 10–30 GPa, UTS = 50–150 MPa, Density ≈ 1.8–2.0 g/cm³.
- **Wolff's Law:** Bone remodels based on mechanical stress. Stress shielding leads to bone resorption.
- **Typical Exam Weightage:** This topic is a **2-mark definition + 7-mark classification** question favorite in Part B of Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metals - Stainless Steel, Co-Cr Alloys, Ti Based Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT614 - BIOMATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metals - Stainless Steel, Co-Cr Alloys, Ti Based Alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Metallic Biomaterials

### Formal Definition
**Metallic biomaterials** are a class of bio-inert (or partially bioactive) engineering materials composed of one or more metallic elements, specifically engineered to interface with biological systems for diagnostic, therapeutic, or replacement purposes. They are widely used as **load-bearing implants** in orthopaedics, dentistry, and cardiovascular applications because of their superior **mechanical strength, ductility, fracture toughness, and fatigue resistance** compared to ceramics or polymers.

The three principal families of metallic biomaterials recognized by **KTU 2024 syllabus** are:
1. **Austenitic Stainless Steels** (mainly **316L SS**)
2. **Cobalt-Chromium (Co-Cr) Alloys** (Cast and Wrought)
3. **Titanium and its Alloys** (CP-Ti and Ti-6Al-4V)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> The module emphasizes the link between **composition → microstructure → mechanical/corrosion behaviour → biological response**. Students are expected to remember standard designations like **ASTM F138, F75, F90** (Co-Cr) and **ASTM F67, F136** (Ti).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bridge" Analogy
Imagine the human skeleton as a busy suspension bridge. When a steel cable (bone) breaks, you cannot replace it with a wooden stick (polymer) or a glass rod (ceramic) — these would be too weak or too brittle. You need a **strong, ductile, fatigue-resistant metal cable** that can withstand millions of cyclic load cycles (heart beats, walking steps) without snapping. Metallic biomaterials serve exactly this purpose — they are the *"metal cables of modern medicine."*

### Why Metals? — The Biocompatibility Triad
A material implanted inside the body must satisfy three competing demands:

| Property Demand | Clinical Reason | Best Material Class |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Strength** | To bear physiological loads (e.g., hip joint ~ 3–5× body weight) | **Metals** |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Body fluid (~ 0.9% NaCl) is a corrosive electrolyte | **Passive metals (Cr/Ti oxide layer)** |
| **Biocompatibility** | No toxicity, allergy, or carcinogenic response | **Noble / passivating metals** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Pure bulk metals are *never* truly inert inside the body. They rely on a thin, adherent, self-healing **passive oxide film** (Cr₂O₃ for SS/Co-Cr, TiO₂ for titanium) to achieve corrosion resistance. Damage to this film → corrosion → metal-ion release → toxicity.

### Standard Metrics to Remember
- **Young's Modulus (E):** ~ 110–230 GPa for common implant metals
- **Density:** ~ 4.5 g/cm³ (Ti) to ~ 8.3 g/cm³ (Co-Cr)
- **Corrosion Rate acceptable limit:** **< 0.01 mm/year** (ASTM G59 standards)
- **Yield Strength (typical implants):** 200–1100 MPa

---

## 1.2 Overview of the Three Metal Families

### 1.2.1 Stainless Steel (316L)
**316L Stainless Steel** (the "L" stands for *Low carbon*, ≤ 0.03% C) is the most widely used **temporary implant** material (bone plates, screws, hip fracture fixation). It is cheap, easily fabricated, and has good short-term biocompatibility, but its high nickel content (~ 10–14%) and susceptibility to **pitting/crevice corrosion** in long-term use limits it to *non-permanent* applications.

### 1.2.2 Cobalt-Chromium (Co-Cr) Alloys
Co-Cr alloys are the **workhorse of dental and orthopaedic load-bearing implants** (e.g., hip stems, knee femoral components, dental bridges). They offer superior **wear resistance, hot strength, and corrosion fatigue life** compared to stainless steel, but are difficult to machine and expensive.

### 1.2.3 Titanium and Ti-6Al-4V
**CP-Ti (Commercially Pure Titanium)** and **Ti-6Al-4V** are the most biocompatible metallic implants. The passive **TiO₂ layer** is highly stable, bio-inert, and even promotes **osseointegration** (direct bone bonding). Their low density (~ 4.5 g/cm³) and low elastic modulus (~ 110 GPa) — closer to bone (~ 17 GPa) — reduce **stress-shielding** effects.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** A common KTU question is *"Why is Ti preferred over SS for long-term implants?"* — Always answer in terms of **(i) passive TiO₂ layer stability, (ii) absence of Ni toxicity, (iii) lower modulus reducing stress-shielding, (iv) better osseointegration.**

---

## 1.3 Revision Anchors for Section 1
- Metals are preferred for **load-bearing** implants.
- Corrosion resistance comes from **passive oxide films**.
- Three main families: **SS, Co-Cr, Ti-alloys**.
- Standards: **ASTM F138 (316L), F75/F90 (Co-Cr), F67/F136 (Ti)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stainless Steel (316L) — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Composition
316L SS is an **austenitic (FCC) stainless steel** stabilized by Nickel. Its typical composition (per **ASTM F138 / F139**):

| Element | Weight % | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Fe** | 60 – 65 % | Base metal (matrix) |
| **Cr** | 17 – 20 % | Forms passive Cr₂O₃ film |
| **Ni** | 10 – 14 % | Stabilizes austenite (FCC) phase |
| **Mo** | 2 – 4 % | Improves pitting resistance |
| **C** | ≤ 0.03 % (Low) | Prevents carbide precipitation at grain boundaries |
| **Mn, Si, P, S** | < 2 % combined | Deoxidizers / impurities |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Low Carbon" (L grade)?** Standard 316 SS has ~ 0.08% C. In the body, chromium carbides (Cr₂₃C₆) precipitate at grain boundaries, depleting the adjacent region of Cr. This is called **sensitization** and causes **intergranular corrosion**. Keeping C ≤ 0.03% prevents this.

### 2.1.2 Microstructure
- **Austenitic (γ-FCC) phase** — non-magnetic, ductile, tough.
- **Grain size** typically ASTM 4–6 (~ 75–100 µm).
- Cold working can transform some austenite to martensite (α′) → increases strength but lowers corrosion resistance.

### 2.1.3 Mechanical Properties (316L SS)
| Property | Value (Annealed) | Value (Cold-worked) |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus (E) | ~ 190 GPa | 190 GPa |
| **Yield Strength (σᵧ)** | 200 – 300 MPa | 600 – 1100 MPa |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength (σᵤₜₛ) | 500 – 600 MPa | 860 – 1350 MPa |
| Elongation | 40 – 55 % | 10 – 20 % |
| Density | 7.9 – 8.0 g/cm³ | — |

### 2.1.4 Corrosion Behaviour
- Passive film: **Cr₂O₃** (~ 1–3 nm thick), self-healing in oxygenated body fluid.
- Limitation: **Pitting corrosion** in Cl⁻-rich environment (body fluid has ~ 0.15 M NaCl).
- **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN):**

$$
\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}
$$

For 316L: PREN ≈ 17 + 3.3(2.5) + 16(0.1) ≈ **25.6** (acceptable but not excellent).

### 2.1.5 Applications & Drawbacks
- **Used in:** bone plates, screws, pins, intramedullary nails, spinal fixators, surgical instruments, orthodontic wires.
- **Drawbacks:** Ni allergy (10–20% of population), stress shielding (high E), not suitable for permanent implants (> 20 years).

---

## 2.2 Cobalt-Chromium (Co-Cr) Alloys — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Two Main Sub-Types

| Sub-Type | Standard | Microstructure | Fabrication | Uses |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cast Co-Cr-Mo** | ASTM F75 | Coarse dendritic carbides (M₂₃C₆) in Co matrix | Investment casting | Hip stems, knee components |
| **Wrought Co-Cr-W-Ni** | ASTM F90 | Carbide-free, work-hardened | Forging / machining | Dental implants, stents |
| **Wrought Co-Ni-Cr-Mo** | ASTM F562 (MP35N) | FCC + HCP dual phase | Cold-worked | Heart valve stents, orthopaedic cables |

### 2.2.2 Composition (ASTM F75 — Cast Co-Cr-Mo)
| Element | Weight % | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Co** | 58 – 69 % | Base metal (matrix) — HCP at RT, FCC at high T |
| **Cr** | 27 – 30 % | Passive Cr₂O₃ formation |
| **Mo** | 5 – 7 % | Strengthens matrix, improves pitting resistance |
| **Ni, Fe, Mn, Si, C** | < 5 % combined | Minor alloying / casting aids |

### 2.2.3 Microstructure & Strengthening
- Co exists in two allotropes: **HCP (ε)** at room temperature, **FCC (γ)** above ~ 417°C.
- **Strengthening mechanisms:**
  1. **Solid solution strengthening** (Mo, W dissolve in Co matrix).
  2. **Carbide precipitation** (M₂₃C₆, M₆C) — increases wear resistance.
  3. **Strain-induced martensite (ε → α′)** transformation in wrought alloys.

### 2.2.4 Mechanical Properties
| Property | Cast Co-Cr-Mo (F75) | Wrought Co-Cr (F90) |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus (E) | 210 – 250 GPa | 210 – 230 GPa |
| Yield Strength (σᵧ) | 450 – 550 MPa | 450 – 950 MPa |
| UTS | 650 – 900 MPa | 900 – 1500 MPa |
| Elongation | 8 – 20 % | 30 – 60 % |
| Density | 8.3 – 8.5 g/cm³ | 8.3 – 8.5 g/cm³ |
| Fatigue Limit | ~ 250 – 300 MPa | ~ 500 – 600 MPa |

### 2.2.5 Corrosion & Wear Resistance
- **Wear resistance** is the best among the three metal families (used in articulating surfaces of hip/knee joints).
- PREN for Co-Cr-Mo: ~ 17 + 3.3(6) ≈ **37** (excellent pitting resistance).
- Forms a very stable **Cr₂O₃ passive film**.

### 2.2.6 Drawbacks
- **High modulus (210–250 GPa)** → significant stress shielding.
- Expensive and difficult to machine (use casting or powder metallurgy).
- Wear debris can cause **metallosis** and osteolysis.
- Co and Cr ions may cause local toxicity (Co²⁺ inhibits osteoblast activity).

---

## 2.3 Titanium and Ti-6Al-4V — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 The Two Main Forms
| Form | Standard | Phases Present | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CP-Ti (Grades 1–4)** | ASTM F67 | Pure α (HCP) | Dental implants, maxillofacial plates |
| **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5)** | ASTM F136 | α + β dual phase | Orthopaedic hip stems, spinal cages, trauma plates |

### 2.3.2 Composition
**CP-Ti (Grade 4):** ≥ 98 % Ti, with controlled O, N, C, H, Fe impurities.
**Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5, ELI):**
- Ti: ~ 89 – 91 %
- Al: 5.5 – 6.5 % (α-stabilizer)
- V: 3.5 – 4.5 % (β-stabilizer)
- Fe, O, N, C, H: < 0.5 % combined

### 2.3.3 Allotropic Behaviour & Phase Stabilization
Titanium exhibits **allotropy**:
- **α-phase:** HCP, stable below 882°C
- **β-phase:** BCC, stable above 882°C

$$
\text{Ti}_{\alpha} \xrightleftharpoons[882^\circ \text{C}]{} \text{Ti}_{\beta}
$$

- **α-stabilizers** (Al, O, N) raise the β-transus.
- **β-stabilizers** (V, Mo, Nb, Fe) lower the β-transus and can retain β at RT.
- Adding 6% Al + 4% V gives an **α + β duplex microstructure** at room temperature.

### 2.3.4 Mechanical Properties
| Property | CP-Ti (Grade 4) | Ti-6Al-4V (Annealed) |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus (E) | ~ 105 GPa | ~ 110 – 114 GPa |
| Yield Strength (σᵧ) | 380 – 550 MPa | 800 – 950 MPa |
| UTS | 480 – 680 MPa | 900 – 1100 MPa |
| Elongation | 15 – 25 % | 8 – 15 % |
| Density | 4.5 g/cm³ | 4.43 g/cm³ |
| Fatigue Limit | ~ 250 MPa | ~ 500 – 600 MPa |

### 2.3.5 Corrosion & Biocompatibility
- Passive film: **TiO₂** (and minor Ti₂O₃, TiO).
- TiO₂ is **bio-inert, non-toxic, and semi-conducting** (band gap ~ 3.0 eV).
- The oxide is highly stable over a wide pH range (1 < pH < 13).
- Ti implants exhibit **osseointegration** — direct chemical bonding with bone via the oxide layer (Per-Ingvar Brånemark, 1965).

### 2.3.6 The Stress-Shielding Issue
Bone, being a piezoelectric tissue, requires mechanical loading (**Wolff's Law**) to maintain density. If a stiff implant shields bone from stress, the bone resorbs.
- Cortical bone: E ≈ 10 – 30 GPa
- SS/Co-Cr: E ≈ 190 – 250 GPa → severe stress shielding
- Ti: E ≈ 110 GPa → better, but still 4–10× bone
- **Modern solution:** β-Ti alloys (e.g., Ti-Nb-Zr-Sn / "Gum Metal") with E ≈ 40–80 GPa

### 2.3.7 Drawbacks of Ti-6Al-4V
- Contains **V** (vanadium) — toxic in ionic form → modern trend: **V-free α + β alloys** (e.g., Ti-6Al-7Nb — ASTM F1295).
- Poor **shear strength** and **tribological (wear) properties** → not preferred for articulating surfaces.
- Difficult to cast and machine.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3\cdot\%\text{Mo} + 16\cdot\%\text{N}$ | Dimensionless; > 32 = "sea-water grade" |
| 2 | Young's Modulus (uniaxial stress) | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | GPa |
| 3 | Hooke's Law (axial loading) | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | MPa |
| 4 | Stress (axial) | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | MPa (N/mm²) |
| 5 | Strain (engineering) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Dimensionless (or %) |
| 6 | Endurance / Fatigue limit estimate (for steels) | $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{\text{UTS}}$ | MPa (rule of thumb) |
| 7 | Density-specific strength (specific strength) | $\sigma_{\text{specific}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{UTS}}}{\rho}$ | kN·m/kg |
| 8 | Modulus-to-bone ratio (stress-shielding indicator) | $R_{sb} = \dfrac{E_{\text{implant}}}{E_{\text{bone}}}$ | Ideally → 1 |
| 9 | Corrosion rate (mass-loss method) | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | mm/year (W in mg, A in cm², t in hours) |
| 10 | Faraday's law of corrosion | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | g; F = 96485 C/mol |
| 11 | ASTM grain size number (n) | $N = 2^{n-1}$ | grains/inch² at 100× |
| 12 | Passivation layer thickness (typical) | $t \approx 1 \text{ to } 10 \text{ nm}$ | nm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **specific strength** ($\sigma/\rho$) determines the *weight efficiency* of an implant — a critical parameter for **aerospace and orthopaedic** applications. Ti-6Al-4V's specific strength (~ 220 kN·m/kg) is the highest among implant metals, explaining its use in both aircraft and bone plates.

---

## 2.5 Comparative Summary Table (Most Important for KTU)

| Property | **316L SS** | **Co-Cr-Mo (F75)** | **Ti-6Al-4V (F136)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 7.9 | 8.3 | **4.5** ✓ |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 190 | 210 – 250 | **110** ✓ |
| UTS (MPa) | 500 – 600 | 650 – 900 | **900 – 1100** ✓ |
| Yield Strength (MPa) | 200 – 300 | 450 – 550 | **800 – 950** ✓ |
| Elongation (%) | 40 – 55 | 8 – 20 | 8 – 15 |
| Corrosion Resistance | Moderate | High | **Highest** ✓ |
| Wear Resistance | Low | **Highest** ✓ | Low |
| Biocompatibility | Moderate (Ni²⁺ risk) | Good (Co²⁺ risk) | **Best** ✓ |
| Osseointegration | Poor | Poor | **Excellent** ✓ |
| Cost | **Lowest** ✓ | High | High |
| MRI Compatibility | Poor (ferromagnetic trace) | Good | **Excellent** ✓ |
| Typical Use | Temporary fixatives | Articulating surfaces | Long-term load-bearing |

---

## 2.6 Concept Anchor: The "Cubic" Mnemonic

To remember the **allotropic crystal structure** of Ti:
- **α (HCP) — "H"eap tightly Packed at low T** (cool → close packing).
- **β (BCC) — "B"ig box at high T** (hot → open, more ductile).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Calculating PREN for 316L vs Co-Cr-Mo

This is a routine KTU calculation-type problem. We compute the Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN) for the two alloys and interpret the result.

**Given:**
- 316L SS composition: 17.5% Cr, 2.4% Mo, 0.08% N
- Co-Cr-Mo (F75) composition: 28% Cr, 6% Mo, 0.20% N (approx.)

**To Find:** Compare PREN values and state which alloy has better pitting resistance.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Recall the PREN formula:

$$
\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 16 \cdot \%\text{N}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values for 316L SS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PREN}_{316L} &= 17.5 + 3.3 \times 2.4 + 16 \times 0.08 \\[4pt]
&= 17.5 + 7.92 + 1.28 \\[4pt]
&= 26.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values for Co-Cr-Mo (F75):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PREN}_{CoCr} &= 28 + 3.3 \times 6 + 16 \times 0.20 \\[4pt]
&= 28 + 19.8 + 3.2 \\[4pt]
&= 51.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret:
- 316L: PREN ≈ 26.7 → "moderate" pitting resistance (acceptable for short-term implants).
- Co-Cr-Mo: PREN ≈ 51.0 → "excellent" pitting resistance (suitable for permanent, articulating implants).
- **Conclusion:** Co-Cr-Mo is ~ 1.9× more pitting-resistant than 316L, primarily due to higher Cr and Mo content.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The "3.3" multiplier for Mo comes from the empirical observation that Mo is 3.3× more effective than Cr in stabilizing the passive film against Cl⁻ attack. The "16" for N reflects its strong effect in forming nitrides that block pit nucleation sites.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Specific Strength of Ti-6Al-4V vs 316L

**Given:**
- Ti-6Al-4V: UTS = 950 MPa, ρ = 4.5 g/cm³ = 4500 kg/m³
- 316L SS: UTS = 580 MPa, ρ = 8.0 g/cm³ = 8000 kg/m³

**To Find:** Specific strength of each, and the ratio.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Specific strength is defined as:

$$
\sigma_{\text{specific}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{UTS}}}{\rho}
$$

**Step 2:** For Ti-6Al-4V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{specific, Ti}} &= \frac{950 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}}{4500 \text{ kg/m}^3} \\[4pt]
&= 211.1 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m/kg} \\[4pt]
&= 211.1 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For 316L SS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{specific, SS}} &= \frac{580 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}}{8000 \text{ kg/m}^3} \\[4pt]
&= 72.5 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m/kg} \\[4pt]
&= 72.5 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{\sigma_{\text{specific, Ti}}}{\sigma_{\text{specific, SS}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{211.1}{72.5} \\[4pt]
&\approx 2.91
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Ti-6Al-4V has nearly **3× the specific strength** of 316L SS, making it the preferred choice for **weight-sensitive implants** (e.g., spinal, dental, maxillofacial).

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Stress-Shielding Modulus Ratio

**Given:**
- Cortical bone: E_bone = 20 GPa
- 316L SS: E = 190 GPa
- Co-Cr: E = 230 GPa
- Ti-6Al-4V: E = 110 GPa

**To Find:** Modulus ratio R_sb for each, and the best implant choice to minimize stress shielding.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Define modulus ratio:

$$
R_{sb} = \frac{E_{\text{implant}}}{E_{\text{bone}}}
$$

**Step 2:** For 316L SS:

$$
R_{sb,\text{SS}} = \frac{190}{20} = 9.50
$$

**Step 3:** For Co-Cr:

$$
R_{sb,\text{CoCr}} = \frac{230}{20} = 11.50
$$

**Step 4:** For Ti-6Al-4V:

$$
R_{sb,\text{Ti}} = \frac{110}{20} = 5.50
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret:
- The closer R_sb → 1, the lower the stress shielding.
- **Ti-6Al-4V (R_sb = 5.5)** is the best among the three, but still 5.5× stiffer than bone.
- New β-Ti alloys (Ti-Nb-Zr-Sn) can achieve R_sb ≈ 2 – 4.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always quote the modulus ratio and end with the conclusion that *"no current implant alloy perfectly matches bone modulus, which is why low-modulus β-Ti alloys are an active research area."*

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4: Corrosion Rate via Mass-Loss Method

**Given:** A 316L SS sample of surface area A = 10 cm², density D = 7.95 g/cm³, exposed for t = 720 hours, loses W = 4.5 mg of mass.

**To Find:** Corrosion rate in mm/year.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Standard mass-loss corrosion rate formula:

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times t}
$$

**Step 2:** Plug in values (W in mg, D in g/cm³, A in cm², t in hours):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR} &= \frac{87.6 \times 4.5}{7.95 \times 10 \times 720} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{394.2}{57240} \\[4pt]
&= 6.89 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm/year} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.0069 \text{ mm/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with the implant acceptance threshold:

$$
\text{CR} = 0.0069 \text{ mm/year} \; < \; 0.01 \text{ mm/year (acceptable limit)} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The 316L SS sample has an acceptable corrosion rate for short-term implant use.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Decision Logic for Implant Selection

| Clinical Scenario | Best Metal | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Temporary bone fracture plate (6–12 months) | **316L SS** | Lowest cost, adequate strength, easy removal |
| Permanent hip joint femoral stem | **Ti-6Al-4V** | Best osseointegration, lowest modulus among metals, MRI safe |
| Articulating surface of knee femoral component | **Co-Cr-Mo (cast F75)** | Highest wear resistance, high PREN |
| Dental root implant | **CP-Ti (Grade 4)** | Best osseointegration, bio-inert, no Ni/V toxicity |
| Cardiovascular stent | **Co-Cr / MP35N (F562)** or **316L SS** | High fatigue strength, MRI-compatible (Co-Cr) |
| Spinal cage / trauma plate | **Ti-6Al-4V** | High specific strength, low density |
| Patient with Ni allergy | **Ti-alloy or Ni-free Co-Cr** | Avoid Ni²⁺ ion release |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
